---
title: "ISA 401: Business Intelligence & Data Visualization"
subtitle: '18: Visualizing High-Dimensional Data in `r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("r-project"), fill = "white")`'
author: '<br>Fadel M. Megahed, PhD <br><br>Raymond E. Gloss Professor in Business <br> Farmer School of Business<br> Miami University<br><br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("linkedin"), fill = "white")` @Fadel Megahed](https://www.linkedin.com/in/fadel-megahed-289046b4) <br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("github"), fill = "white")` fmegahed](https://github.com/fmegahed/) <br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("paper-plane", style = "solid"), fill = "white")` fmegahed@miamioh.edu](mailto:fmegahed@miamioh.edu)<br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("question"), fill = "white")` Automated Scheduler for Office Hours](https://calendly.com/fmegahed)<br><br>'
date: "Spring 2026"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    self_contained: true
    css: [default, "../../style_files/fonts.css", "../../style_files/my-theme.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: magula
      highlightLines: true
      highlightLanguage: ["r"]
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: "16:9"
header-includes:
  - "../../style_files/header.html"
---


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache = FALSE,
                      echo = TRUE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      progress = FALSE,
                      verbose = FALSE,
                      dev = 'png',
                      fig.height = 3,
                      dpi = 300,
                      fig.align = 'center')

options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE)

miamired = '#C3142D'

if(require(pacman)==FALSE) install.packages("pacman")
if(require(devtools)==FALSE) install.packages("devtools")

if(require(countdown)==FALSE) devtools::install_github("gadenbuie/countdown")
if(require(xaringanExtra)==FALSE) devtools::install_github("gadenbuie/xaringanExtra")


pacman::p_load(tidyverse, magrittr, lubridate, janitor, # data analysis pkgs
               DT, # for nicely printed output
               GGally, ggradar, pheatmap, scales, # for high-dimensional charts
               DataExplorer,
               fontawesome, RefManageR, xaringanExtra, countdown) # for slides
```

```{r xaringan-themer, include=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
if(require(xaringanthemer) == FALSE) install.packages("xaringanthemer")
library(xaringanthemer)

style_mono_accent(base_color = "#84d6d3",
                  base_font_size = "20px")

xaringanExtra::use_xaringan_extra(c("tile_view", "animate_css", "tachyons", "panelset", "search", "fit_screen", "editable",
                                    "clipable"))
```


## Quick Refresher from Last Week


`r emo::ji("check")` Identify common text-visualization techniques (word clouds, bar charts of term frequency).

`r emo::ji("check")` Create word clouds and network graphs from text data in `r fontawesome::fa("r-project", fill = miamired)`.

`r emo::ji("check")` Apply sentiment analysis and visualize sentiment scores.

---

## Kahoot/Quiz Review: Text Data Visualization

.pull-left[

**Q1.** Which function from the `tidytext` package splits text into individual words?
A. `str_split()`
B. `unnest_tokens()`
C. `strsplit()`
D. `tokenize()`

**Q2.** In a word cloud, what visual property encodes term frequency?
A. Color hue
B. Position on the x-axis
C. Font size
D. Font style (bold/italic)

]

.pull-right[

**Q3.** Which sentiment lexicon assigns words a numeric score from -5 to +5?
A. Bing
B. NRC
C. AFINN
D. Loughran

**Q4.** What does a network graph of text data typically show?
A. Sentiment over time
B. Co-occurrence of words
C. Word frequency per document
D. Topic distributions

]


`r countdown(minutes = 3, warn_when = 60, color_background = miamired, color_text = "white", font_size = "1.5em", top = 0, right = 0)`


---

# Learning Objectives for Today's Class

- **LO1:** Define high-dimensional data and describe its visualization challenges

- **LO2:** Create parallel coordinates plots, radar charts, and heatmaps in `r fontawesome::fa("r-project", fill = miamired)`

- **LO3:** Use small multiples and faceting to compare distributions across categories


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# What is High-Dimensional Data?

---

# Why Does Dimensionality Matter?

.pull-left[

Most of the charts we have built so far encode **two or three variables** at most:

- A scatter plot uses **x** and **y**
- A bar chart uses a **category** and a **count/value**
- A line chart uses **time** and a **metric**

**But real business data is rarely that simple.**

A single job-market dataset might have columns for salary, experience, skills count, industry, location, education level, company size, and more.

]

.pull-right[

.content-box-grey[

.font120[**The Core Challenge**]

We live in a 2-D display world. How do we faithfully represent data that lives in 10, 50, or 100+ dimensions?

> "The greatest value of a picture is when it forces us to notice what we never expected to see." -- John Tukey

]

]


---

# Defining Key Terms

`r countdown(minutes = 3, seconds = 0, top = 0, font_size = "2em")`

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Activity]

> In 3 minutes, define the terms in the next tab using the sample data below as context.

.font70[
```{r job_data_preview, echo=FALSE}
jobs_data <- tibble::tribble(
  ~title,              ~salary, ~experience, ~skills_count, ~industry,      ~education,   ~company_size,
  "Data Analyst",       65000,  2,           5,             "Tech",         "Bachelors",  "Large",

  "BI Developer",       78000,  4,           8,             "Finance",      "Masters",    "Medium",
  "Data Scientist",     95000,  3,           12,            "Healthcare",   "PhD",        "Large",
  "Analyst Intern",     35000,  0,           3,             "Consulting",   "Bachelors",  "Small",
  "ML Engineer",       110000,  5,           15,            "Tech",         "Masters",    "Large",
  "Report Developer",   60000,  2,           6,             "Manufacturing","Bachelors",  "Medium"
)
DT::datatable(jobs_data, rownames = FALSE, options = list(pageLength = 6, dom = 'tip'))
```
]

]

.panel[.panel-name[Your Definitions]

.can-edit.key-activity1[

**Data Types:** .font70[(Edit below)]

- Multivariate data: ________________

- Big Data: ________________

- Tall Data: ________________

- Wide Data: ________________

- High Dimensional Data: ________________

]
]
]


---

# Taxonomy of Dimensionality

**Based on the number of attributes (columns/variables):**

- **1:** Univariate (histogram, boxplot)
- **2:** Bivariate (scatter plot, line chart)
- **3:** Trivariate (bubble chart, 3-D scatter)
- **4+:** Multivariate / High-Dimensional

**Key questions to think about:**

- .can-edit.key-activity2a[What is the problem with visualizing multivariate data (especially when $p > 6-7$ dimensions)? ______________]

- .can-edit.key-activity2b[Any ideas about what to do? ______________]


---

# Encoding Mechanisms: How We Map Variables to Visuals

When we have many variables, we need **multiple encoding channels**:

| Channel | Best For | Example |
|---------|----------|---------|
| **x-axis** | Numeric / ordinal variable | GDP per capita |
| **y-axis** | Numeric / ordinal variable | Life expectancy |
| **Area / size** | Numeric variable | Population |
| **Color hue** | Nominal / categorical | Continent |
| **Color intensity** | Numeric / ordinal | Growth rate |
| **Shape** | Nominal (limited to ~6) | Country group |
| **Motion / animation** | Time | Year |
| **Faceting** | Categorical | Region subsets |

> The more channels we use simultaneously, the more dimensions we can display -- but cognitive load increases.


---

# Hans Rosling: The Best Stats You Have Seen

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Watch the Video]

> While watching this video, answer the questions in the next tab.

<center>
<div style="max-width:600px"><div style="position:relative;height:0;padding-bottom:56.25%"><iframe src="https://embed.ted.com/talks/lang/en/hans_rosling_the_best_stats_you_ve_ever_seen" width="600" height="350" style="position:absolute;left:0;top:0;width:100%;height:100%" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" allowfullscreen></iframe></div></div>
</center>

]

.panel[.panel-name[Your Answers]

- .can-edit.key-activity3a[What data is represented in this visualization? Be specific. ____]

- .can-edit.key-activity3b[How is each data type visually encoded (x, y, size, color, motion)? ___________]

- .can-edit.key-activity3c[Do you think the encodings are appropriate? Why or why not? _________]

]
]

---

# What Makes the Gapminder Chart Work?

> Motion charts are essentially **animated bubble charts**. A bubble chart shows data using the .bold[x-axis], .bold[y-axis], and the .bold[size] and .bold[color] of the bubble. A motion chart displays .red[.bold[changes over time by showing movement within the two-dimensional space and changes in the size and color of the bubbles]]. -- [Juice Analytics](https://www.juiceanalytics.com/writing/better-know-visualization-motion-charts)

**Encoding mechanisms in the Gapminder chart:**

- .bold[x-axis]: GDP per capita (**numeric**)
- .bold[y-axis]: Life expectancy (**numeric**)
- .bold[area]: Population (**numeric**)
- .bold[color]: Continent (**nominal**)
- .bold[motion/animation]: Year (**time**)

That is **five dimensions** on a single 2-D screen -- powerful and intuitive.

<iframe src="https://www.gapminder.org/tools/?embedded=true#$chart-type=bubbles&url=v1" style="width: 100%; height: 250px; margin: 0; border: 1px solid grey;" allowfullscreen></iframe>


---

## LO1 Check-In

`r emo::ji("check")` You can now:

- Define multivariate, big, tall, wide, and high-dimensional data

- Explain why visualizing many variables simultaneously is challenging

- Identify the encoding channels available for mapping variables to visuals


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Parallel Coordinates, Radar Charts & Heatmaps

---

# Parallel Coordinates Plots

> Parallel coordinates is a visualization technique used to plot individual data elements across many performance measures. Each measure corresponds to a **vertical axis** and each data element is displayed as a **series of connected points** along the axes. -- [Juice Analytics](https://www.juiceanalytics.com/writing/writing/parallel-coordinates)

.pull-left[

**When to use:**
- Comparing many numeric variables simultaneously
- Spotting clusters, outliers, and correlations
- Exploring patterns across job profiles, products, etc.

**Key considerations:**
- Variable **order** matters -- try different arrangements
- **Scaling** is critical (use standardization)
- Too many lines can create visual clutter

]

.pull-right[

```{r parcoord-concept, echo=FALSE, out.height=280}
knitr::include_graphics('https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/52f42657e4b0b3416ff6b831/1614951876498-9TIQE0EHNCGUFQSNEP6J/Parallel_Coordinates_Plot_-_Learn_about_this_chart_and_tools.jpg?format=1000w')
```

]


---

# Demo: Parallel Coordinates with `GGally::ggparcoord()`

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Create Data]

```{r parcoord-data}
job_profiles <- tibble::tribble(
  ~role,               ~salary_k, ~skills, ~experience, ~satisfaction, ~growth_pct,
  "Data Analyst",       65,        5,       2,           7.5,           12,
  "BI Developer",       78,        8,       4,           7.0,           10,
  "Data Scientist",     95,       12,       3,           8.5,           22,
  "ML Engineer",       110,       15,       5,           8.0,           28,
  "Report Developer",   60,        6,       2,           6.5,            8,
  "Analytics Manager",  92,       10,       7,           7.8,           15,
  "Data Engineer",     100,       11,       4,           7.2,           25,
  "Statistician",       80,        9,       3,           7.0,           11
)

job_profiles
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Plot Code]

```{r parcoord-plot, fig.height=3.5, fig.width=9, eval=FALSE}
GGally::ggparcoord(
  data = job_profiles,
  columns = 2:6,            # numeric columns
  groupColumn = 1,          # color by role
  scale = "std",            # standardize to mean 0, sd 1
  showPoints = TRUE
) +
  ggplot2::scale_color_brewer(palette = "Paired") +
  ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(size = 3) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "top",
        axis.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1)) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Variable", y = "Standardized Value",
       title = "Parallel Coordinates: Job Role Profiles",
       color = "Role")
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Output]

```{r parcoord-plot-run, fig.height=3.5, fig.width=9, echo=FALSE}
GGally::ggparcoord(
  data = job_profiles,
  columns = 2:6,
  groupColumn = 1,
  scale = "std",
  showPoints = TRUE
) +
  ggplot2::scale_color_brewer(palette = "Paired") +
  ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(size = 3) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "top",
        axis.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1)) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Variable", y = "Standardized Value",
       title = "Parallel Coordinates: Job Role Profiles",
       color = "Role")
```

]
]


---

# Radar Charts

> Radar charts (also called spider or web charts) show how individual items perform **across multiple measures** arranged radially. Each axis represents a variable, and the data point on each axis connects to form a polygon.

.pull-left[

**When to use:**
- Comparing a **small number** of items (2-5) across several metrics
- Showing strengths and weaknesses of profiles
- Presenting skill assessments or balanced scorecards

**Limitations:**
- Hard to read with many items or many axes
- Area can be misleading (depends on variable order)
- Not ideal for precise comparisons

]

.pull-right[

```{r radar-concept, echo=FALSE, out.height=280}
knitr::include_graphics('https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/52f42657e4b0b3416ff6b831/1633047594333-7OEOYF4YZU4TMI2VMJSR/A_Night_Under_The_Stars___Jordan_Vincent.png?format=1000w')
```

]


---

# Demo: Radar Chart with `ggradar`

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Prepare Data]

```{r radar-data}
# ggradar expects: first column = group, remaining columns = values in [0, 1]
skill_profiles <- tibble::tribble(
  ~role,            ~R, ~Python, ~SQL, ~Tableau, ~Excel, ~Stats,
  "Data Analyst",   0.6,  0.3,   0.8,   0.7,     0.9,    0.5,
  "Data Scientist",  0.9,  0.9,   0.7,   0.5,     0.4,    0.9,
  "BI Developer",   0.4,  0.2,   0.9,   0.9,     0.7,    0.3,
  "ML Engineer",    0.7,  0.95,  0.6,   0.3,     0.2,    0.8
)

skill_profiles
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Plot Code]

```{r radar-plot, fig.height=4, fig.width=8, eval=FALSE}
ggradar::ggradar(
  skill_profiles,
  grid.min = 0, grid.mid = 0.5, grid.max = 1,
  values.radar = c("0", "0.5", "1"),
  group.line.width = 1.2,
  group.point.size = 3,
  legend.position = "bottom",
  legend.text.size = 10,
  axis.label.size = 4
) +
  ggplot2::labs(title = "Skill Profiles Across Job Roles")
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Output]

```{r radar-plot-run, fig.height=4, fig.width=8, echo=FALSE}
ggradar::ggradar(
  skill_profiles,
  grid.min = 0, grid.mid = 0.5, grid.max = 1,
  values.radar = c("0", "0.5", "1"),
  group.line.width = 1.2,
  group.point.size = 3,
  legend.position = "bottom",
  legend.text.size = 10,
  axis.label.size = 4
) +
  ggplot2::labs(title = "Skill Profiles Across Job Roles")
```

]
]


---

# Heatmaps

.pull-left[

**What is a heatmap?**

- A matrix visualization where **color intensity** encodes the value in each cell
- Rows and columns represent categories or observations
- Useful for spotting **patterns, clusters, and outliers** in large matrices

**When to use:**
- Skill-by-industry matrices
- Correlation matrices
- Time-of-day / day-of-week patterns

]

.pull-right[

**Key considerations:**
- Choose a **sequential** color scale for single-direction data (e.g., 0 to max)
- Choose a **diverging** color scale for data with a meaningful midpoint (e.g., correlations from -1 to +1)
- **Ordering** rows/columns (e.g., by clustering) reveals structure

]


---

# Demo: Heatmap with `pheatmap`

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Create Matrix]

```{r heatmap-data}
# Skill demand matrix: industries x skills
skill_demand <- tibble::tribble(
  ~industry,       ~R, ~Python, ~SQL, ~Tableau, ~Excel, ~ML, ~Cloud,
  "Tech",           9,    9,     8,     5,       3,      9,    8,
  "Finance",        6,    7,     9,     7,       8,      5,    4,
  "Healthcare",     5,    6,     7,     6,       7,      4,    3,
  "Consulting",     4,    5,     6,     8,       9,      3,    3,
  "Manufacturing",  3,    4,     5,     7,       9,      2,    2,
  "Retail",         3,    5,     7,     8,       8,      4,    5
)

# Convert to matrix for pheatmap
skill_matrix <- skill_demand |> dplyr::select(-industry) |> as.matrix()
rownames(skill_matrix) <- skill_demand$industry
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Plot Code]

```{r heatmap-plot, fig.height=3.5, fig.width=7, eval=FALSE}
pheatmap::pheatmap(
  skill_matrix,
  color = colorRampPalette(c("white", "#84d6d3", miamired))(50),
  cluster_rows = TRUE,
  cluster_cols = TRUE,
  display_numbers = TRUE,
  number_format = "%d",
  fontsize = 11,
  main = "Skill Demand by Industry (1-10 Scale)"
)
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Output]

```{r heatmap-plot-run, fig.height=3.5, fig.width=7, echo=FALSE}
pheatmap::pheatmap(
  skill_matrix,
  color = colorRampPalette(c("white", "#84d6d3", miamired))(50),
  cluster_rows = TRUE,
  cluster_cols = TRUE,
  display_numbers = TRUE,
  number_format = "%d",
  fontsize = 11,
  main = "Skill Demand by Industry (1-10 Scale)"
)
```

]
]


---

# Bonus: Correlation Heatmap

A common use case for heatmaps is visualizing **correlation matrices** -- this instantly reveals which variables move together:

```{r cor-heatmap, fig.height=3.5, fig.width=6}
# Using our job_profiles data
cor_matrix <- job_profiles |>
  dplyr::select(-role) |>
  cor()

pheatmap::pheatmap(
  cor_matrix,
  color = colorRampPalette(c(miamired, "white", "#84d6d3"))(50),
  display_numbers = TRUE,
  number_format = "%.2f",
  fontsize = 11,
  main = "Correlation Matrix: Job Profile Variables"
)
```

> **Reading tip:** Strong positive correlations (near +1) appear in teal; strong negative correlations (near -1) appear in red.


---

# When to Use Which Chart?

| Chart | Best For | Max Items | Limitations |
|-------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Parallel Coordinates** | Comparing many numeric variables | 20-30 lines | Clutter with too many observations |
| **Radar Chart** | Comparing profiles | 3-5 items | Misleading areas; hard to read with many axes |
| **Heatmap** | Matrix data / correlations | Unlimited rows & cols | Requires good color scale and ordering |
| **Bubble Chart** | 4-5 variables | 50-100 points | Overlapping bubbles |
| **Small Multiples** | Comparing distributions | 12-20 panels | Space-intensive |


---

## Activity: Create a Parallel Coordinates Plot

Using the `job_profiles` data from our earlier demo (or your own data), create a parallel coordinates plot that:

1. Groups lines by `role`
2. Uses `scale = "uniminmax"` (scales each variable to [0, 1])
3. Includes points and a clean theme
4. Has a descriptive title

```{r activity-parcoord-starter, eval=FALSE}
# Start with the job_profiles data
GGally::ggparcoord(
  data = job_profiles,
  columns = ___,
  groupColumn = ___,
  scale = "uniminmax",
  showPoints = ___
) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw() +
  ggplot2::labs(title = "___")
```

`r countdown(minutes = 5, warn_when = 60, color_background = miamired, color_text = "white", font_size = "1.5em", top = 0, right = 0)`


---

## LO2 Check-In

`r emo::ji("check")` You can now create high-dimensional visualizations in `r fontawesome::fa("r-project", fill = miamired)`:

- **Parallel coordinates** with `GGally::ggparcoord()` for comparing numeric profiles

- **Radar charts** with `ggradar::ggradar()` for comparing a few items across metrics

- **Heatmaps** with `pheatmap::pheatmap()` for matrix data with color-encoded values


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Small Multiples & Faceting

---

# The Principle of Small Multiples

> "Illustrations of postage-stamp size are indexed by category or a label, sequenced over time like the frames of a movie, or ordered by a quantitative variable not used in the single image itself." -- Edward Tufte, *Envisioning Information* (1990)

.pull-left[

**Why small multiples work:**
- Same chart structure repeated across subsets
- Easy to compare because the **scale is consistent**
- The eye can scan for differences quickly
- Avoids overplotting and clutter

]

.pull-right[

```{r small-multiples-example, echo=FALSE, out.width='95%'}
knitr::include_graphics('https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/52f42657e4b0b3416ff6b831/1614444947046-W4UOZ7ISU9IV4CG8URSU/FIres-detected-in-Amazon.png?format=1000w')
```

.font70[Source: NYT analysis of Amazon rain forest fires]

]


---

# Demo: `facet_wrap()` for Salary Distributions

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Create Data]

```{r facet-data}
set.seed(401)
jobs_facet <- tibble::tibble(
  industry = rep(c("Tech", "Finance", "Healthcare", "Consulting"), each = 60),
  experience = rep(rep(c("Entry", "Mid", "Senior"), each = 20), 4),
  salary_k = c(
    rnorm(20, 65, 10), rnorm(20, 85, 12), rnorm(20, 110, 15),  # Tech
    rnorm(20, 60, 8),  rnorm(20, 80, 10), rnorm(20, 105, 14),  # Finance
    rnorm(20, 55, 9),  rnorm(20, 72, 11), rnorm(20, 95, 13),   # Healthcare
    rnorm(20, 58, 8),  rnorm(20, 75, 10), rnorm(20, 100, 12)   # Consulting
  )
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(experience = factor(experience, levels = c("Entry", "Mid", "Senior")))
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[facet_wrap Code]

```{r facet-wrap-plot, fig.height=3.5, fig.width=10, eval=FALSE}
ggplot2::ggplot(jobs_facet, ggplot2::aes(x = salary_k, fill = experience)) +
  ggplot2::geom_histogram(bins = 12, alpha = 0.7, color = "white") +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ industry, nrow = 1) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("Entry" = "#84d6d3", "Mid" = "#f4a261", "Senior" = miamired)) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "top") +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Salary ($ thousands)", y = "Count",
       title = "Salary Distributions by Industry and Experience Level",
       fill = "Experience")
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Output]

```{r facet-wrap-plot-run, fig.height=3.5, fig.width=10, echo=FALSE}
ggplot2::ggplot(jobs_facet, ggplot2::aes(x = salary_k, fill = experience)) +
  ggplot2::geom_histogram(bins = 12, alpha = 0.7, color = "white") +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ industry, nrow = 1) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("Entry" = "#84d6d3", "Mid" = "#f4a261", "Senior" = miamired)) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "top") +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Salary ($ thousands)", y = "Count",
       title = "Salary Distributions by Industry and Experience Level",
       fill = "Experience")
```

]
]


---

# `facet_grid()`: Two-Dimensional Faceting

When you want to facet by **two variables simultaneously**, use `facet_grid(rows ~ cols)`:

```{r facet-grid, fig.height=3.5, fig.width=10, eval=FALSE}
ggplot2::ggplot(jobs_facet, ggplot2::aes(x = salary_k)) +
  ggplot2::geom_density(fill = "#84d6d3", alpha = 0.5) +
  ggplot2::facet_grid(experience ~ industry) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Salary ($ thousands)", y = "Density",
       title = "Salary Density by Industry (columns) and Experience Level (rows)")
```

```{r facet-grid-run, fig.height=3.5, fig.width=10, echo=FALSE}
ggplot2::ggplot(jobs_facet, ggplot2::aes(x = salary_k)) +
  ggplot2::geom_density(fill = "#84d6d3", alpha = 0.5) +
  ggplot2::facet_grid(experience ~ industry) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Salary ($ thousands)", y = "Density",
       title = "Salary Density by Industry (columns) and Experience Level (rows)")
```


---

# Quick Alternative: `DataExplorer::plot_boxplot()`

For rapid exploration, `DataExplorer` can generate faceted boxplots in a single line:

```{r data-explorer-box, fig.height=3, fig.width=9, eval=FALSE}
DataExplorer::plot_boxplot(
  data = jobs_facet,
  by = "industry",
  theme_config = ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 10)
)
```

.font80[
This is especially useful when you have **many numeric variables** and want to quickly see how they distribute across a grouping variable -- no manual faceting required.
]

```{r data-explorer-box-run, fig.height=3, fig.width=9, echo=FALSE}
DataExplorer::plot_boxplot(
  data = jobs_facet,
  by = "industry",
  theme_config = ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 10)
)
```


---

## Activity: Small Multiples with Faceting

Using the `jobs_facet` data (or a dataset of your choice), create a faceted visualization that shows:

1. **Boxplots** of salary by experience level
2. **Faceted** by industry using `facet_wrap()`
3. A color scheme that distinguishes experience levels

```{r activity-facet-starter, eval=FALSE}
ggplot2::ggplot(jobs_facet, ggplot2::aes(x = ___, y = ___, fill = ___)) +
  ggplot2::geom_boxplot() +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ ___) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw() +
  ggplot2::labs(title = "___")
```

`r countdown(minutes = 5, warn_when = 60, color_background = miamired, color_text = "white", font_size = "1.5em", top = 0, right = 0)`


---

## LO3 Check-In

`r emo::ji("check")` You can now use small multiples and faceting by:

- Applying `facet_wrap()` to split a chart by one categorical variable

- Applying `facet_grid()` to split by two categorical variables (rows and columns)

- Using `DataExplorer::plot_boxplot()` for quick faceted exploration


---

# The Big Picture: Choosing the Right Tool

.content-box-grey[
.font120[**Real business data has 10, 50, 100+ variables -- you need tools to see it all.**]

The key insight is that **no single chart handles all dimensions**. Instead, you build a **toolkit**:

- **Parallel coordinates** for scanning across many numeric variables at once
- **Radar charts** for focused comparisons of a few profiles
- **Heatmaps** for spotting patterns in matrix data
- **Small multiples** for comparing distributions across categories
- **Bubble/motion charts** for encoding 4-5 variables with animation
]

> The best analysts are not those who memorize chart types, but those who can **match the question to the visual encoding** that answers it most clearly.


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Recap

---

## Summary of Main Points

By now, you should be able to do the following:

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO1:** Define high-dimensional data and explain why standard 2-D charts are insufficient for multivariate datasets.

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO2:** Create parallel coordinates plots (`GGally::ggparcoord()`), radar charts (`ggradar::ggradar()`), and heatmaps (`pheatmap::pheatmap()`) in `r fontawesome::fa("r-project", fill = miamired)`.

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO3:** Use `facet_wrap()` and `facet_grid()` for small multiples, and `DataExplorer::plot_boxplot()` for rapid exploration.


---

## Key Functions Reference

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `GGally::ggparcoord()` | Create parallel coordinates plots |
| `ggradar::ggradar()` | Create radar/spider charts |
| `pheatmap::pheatmap()` | Create clustered heatmaps |
| `ggplot2::facet_wrap()` | Facet a plot by one variable |
| `ggplot2::facet_grid()` | Facet by two variables (rows x cols) |
| `DataExplorer::plot_boxplot()` | Quick faceted boxplots by a grouping variable |
| `scales::rescale()` | Rescale numeric values to [0, 1] |


---

## Readings and Resources

- [GGally package documentation](https://ggobi.github.io/ggally/) -- Comprehensive guide to `ggparcoord()` and other GGally functions.

- [ggradar GitHub repository](https://github.com/ricardo-bion/ggradar) -- Examples and usage for radar charts in ggplot2.

- [pheatmap CRAN page](https://cran.r-project.org/package=pheatmap) -- Documentation for the `pheatmap` package.

- [Tufte, E.R. (1990). *Envisioning Information*.](https://www.amazon.com/Envisioning-Information-Edward-R-Tufte/dp/0961392118) -- The definitive text on small multiples and information design.

- [Hans Rosling TED Talk](https://www.ted.com/talks/hans_rosling_the_best_stats_you_ve_ever_seen) -- The iconic talk demonstrating motion bubble charts.


---

## Things to Do to Prepare for Next Class

- **Review:** Experiment with `ggparcoord()`, `ggradar()`, and `pheatmap()` on a dataset of your choice.

- **Explore:** Try the [Gapminder interactive tool](https://www.gapminder.org/tools/) and identify how each variable is encoded.

- **Reflect:** For your project dataset, which high-dimensional visualization technique would be most appropriate? Why?

- **Next class:** Data Storytelling & Dashboards -- turning charts into compelling narratives.
